So I will go to the environment variables folder and open with code. And I can run my app by typing npm run dev. npm run dev And you see my app will start here. No surprises whatsoever. So you see our app has started here. Now let's talk about environment variables. What are environment variables? So if I make a file named .env here. And I write here let us say secret secret secret is equal to and I write here harry123456 And let's say this is our email id. I will write here id is equal to harry So I have stored some credentials here. Now why didn't we hard code it directly in the app? Why are we storing it separately here? The reason is that these credentials can be changed. Or they can be configurable variables. Or they can be so secret that I will push my code on github. So I don't want to push these credentials on github. For example, when we make AI projects, then the key of open AI is used in it. So I don't want my open AI key to be pushed here. So we separate things. There are two reasons. One is that we want to change it again and again. Where will we keep looking in the code? Where is the key? Where is the secret? Where will we keep finding and replacing? We have written it here and we can use it in our code. Now I will show you how to use this id and secret. So if I want to use id and secret, then I can do console.log here. Note that this is a server component. I will say make it a client component. Now you guys keep in mind that when I make it a client component, I will comment this line because nothing is working on the server. Now I wrote here hey this is home the id is process.env.id and secret is process.env.secret Now when we were on the server then process.env was loaded and this file was loaded. All variables were loaded on the server. Because server is a component. Page is being made on the server. These variables are on the server. But when we are on the client, when we wrote here use client, we are on the client side and because we are on the client side that's why the process.env will be client process.env and as a result, you see the id is nothing, the secret is nothing. So what happened? Why didn't it come? We made it a client component so it didn't come. Everything was coming and working properly. That's why everything was going well because process.env is loaded on the server. It's not loaded on the client. In Next.js the environment of server side and client side is different. So this process.env will be client's process.env i.e. browser's process.env in which it won't get neither id nor secret because all this is on the server. So isn't there any way to put our id here and it will show here. Suppose I want the id to be visible on the browser and do you know why it was done? So that your environment variables don't get exposed by mistake. All environment variables are not exposed. You have to do next underscore public underscore and as soon as you write next underscore public underscore id is equal to harry then you can access it here and I will tell you why it happens. So you see here id is harry is written. When we write next underscore public underscore id, process.env dot, it is written. So next JS automatically starts the environment variables from next underscore public underscore and sends all those environment variables to the browser. Which means that even if I remove these use lines from here then my server side will also work perfectly. And you have seen on the client side that it was doing. So if you want these environment variables to be a part of your JavaScript bundle which goes to the client side client side if you have a client component then through the JavaScript bundle process.env. if you want this environment variable to be accessed through the client and through the server then put next underscore public. So by doing this it will become a public environment variable which means it will be a part of the JavaScript bundle. But note that you don't do this for sensitive variables. Your database has a password. No, don't make next underscore public. So attackers will remove it from the JavaScript bundle. If you feel that I am not displaying here then it will become a part of the JavaScript bundle. And you will become vulnerable for the attack. So I don't want you to be vulnerable for the attack. Now we talked about .env. So the environment variables that was pretty much it. But now let's talk a little more advanced. It's simple. It's advanced but simple. So if I make a file here .env.local and I write here next public id is equal to Rohan and I write here Rohan123456 or Rohan3456 So I make it a server component and after making a server component you see that Rohan and Rohan 3456 are both being printed. So .env.local takes more priority. If there is a variable in it, will it be loaded? Yes. So I will tell you how it is loaded and why this file is made like this. Whenever you are making an app you are working on a local environment and along with that you are working in a production environment where you push your code etc. So in this way your local environment variables are different and your production environment variables are different. For example, if you are using MongoDB then in production your MongoDB credential is different. Your URI is different. It is different in local. So the local if you want to put something different in it, if you want to put something different in production then you make .env.local first. So .env.local is added first and after that .env is given priority. If .env.local is not present then .env's environment variables are loaded. Now what we will do is we will go to the documentation of Next.js and we will see the priority that how it is loaded. So I will search Next.js environment variables and here is a page of environment variables. A lot of information is given in this including the next public information that we already talked about. But what I want is that we read only those things which are important. Now see this line that what is the order of load that in which order environment variables are loaded. First of all, your process.env will be loaded. That means operating system environment variables are not there. So .env. suppose your development server is development.local then .env.local will be loaded. And after that .env. your node.env which can be development or production or .env after that priority is given to it. Now here the values of node.env are production, development and test. This is good to know. At the same time let me tell you that you don't need to remember all this. I will be very honest with you. You don't need to remember all this. The reason for that is that mostly 95% of the time you are going to use .env.local or .env. So you should know that .env.local is there. So that will be prioritized first. And if it is not there and there is no variable in it then it will be loaded from .env. Like suppose I make a name environment variable and suppose I put name shubham and if I try to access the name. Suppose I write here and name is process.env. and here I write name. So if I do this then what will happen is that shubham will come here and he has loaded it from .env.local and this shubham has loaded it from .env. Because it was not present here. So mostly you need to know this that .env.local will be read first whatever is found is found. Whatever is not found then it will be loaded from .env. If it is present then. So that was about environment variables. According to me, you only need to know this There is a lot of advanced stuff but this was pretty much gist of this page which I told you in this video. Next underscore public is really very important. You guys understand it well. For the rest when we make projects and on this channel you will get to see a lot of things.
